text: The surgeon releases adhesions between the left colon and sigmoid colon from the wall of the deep pelvis, carefully separating these structures to facilitate further surgical access.
The surgical team gains access to the left iliac vessels and the left ureter, likely through the release of adhesions and strategic port placement.
The surgeon begins the procedure by making an incision in the peritoneum lateral to the left colon, using surgical instruments to access underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter, mobilizes it, and divides it at the intramural tunnel, using precise dissection techniques to separate the ureter from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully preserves periureteral tissue while working around the left ureter, ensuring minimal disruption to surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies a hemolock clip to the ureter at its insertion point into the bladder, securing the structure before division.
The surgeon clips the proximal ureter using a pre-tied suture attached to a hemolock clip, preparing the ureter for later manipulation.
A small margin of the ureter is excised and prepared to be sent for frozen section analysis.
The surgeon mobilizes the ureter to the point where it crosses the iliac vessels, ensuring it has sufficient length for crossing over to the right side for future urinary diversion.
The surgeon isolates the right ureter using a similar approach as previously described, ensuring careful dissection and preservation of surrounding periureteral tissue.
The surgeon develops the pre-rectal and posterior vesicle space, carefully dissecting the tissue layers to expose these anatomical areas.
The surgeon identifies the pouch of Douglas and incises the anterior peritoneum along the posterior surface of the bladder, near the tips of the seminal vesicles.
The surgeon incises Denonvilliers' fascia and dissects in the posterior plane, separating the rectum from the bladder and prostate by following the longitudinal fibers of the rectum.
The surgeon continues the dissection caudally towards the apex of the prostate, ensuring the separation of anatomical structures in this region.
The surgeon performs a left lymphadenectomy, carefully excising lymphatic tissue from the left pelvic region using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon employs a split and roll technique to manipulate and separate tissue layers, likely within the pre-rectal and posterior vesicle space.
The surgeon performs an extended pelvic lymph node dissection between the external and internal iliac vessels, carefully separating lymphatic tissue from surrounding structures.

The surgeon identifies and dissects additional lymph nodes, including the external iliac, pre-sacral, common iliac, paracaval, and para-aortic nodes, as part of the extended pelvic lymph node dissection.

The surgeon performs contralateral nodal dissection using a split and roll technique, similar to the previous dissection, focusing on the lymph nodes adjacent to the external and internal iliac vessels.
After completing the nodal dissection, the surgeon visualizes and divides the pedicles of the bladder and prostate, clearly exposing these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a ligature to divide the bladder pedicles bilaterally, with the pedicles clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects to the endopelvic fascia and incises it bilaterally, exposing the prostate and its apex.
The surgeon develops the space of Retzius following posterior lateral dissection, revealing the anatomical area between the pubic symphysis and the bladder.
The surgeon cauterizes the superficial venous complex and secures the deep venous complex with sutures, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the deep venous complex proximal to the previously placed suture, isolating the urethra from surrounding structures.
The surgeon transects the urethra, revealing the Foley catheter within the surgical field.
The surgeon clips and divides the Foley catheter, which is visible after the transection of the urethra.
The specimen is rotated with the Foley catheter pointing anteriorly to prevent urine spillage, following its liberation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins the creation of an ileal conduit, focusing on the preparation and manipulation of the ileum for urinary diversion.
The surgeon passes the left ureter behind the mesentery of the sigmoid colon, positioning it for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the sigmoid mesentery to enlarge the window, providing better access for the surgical procedure.
The right-side grasper is used to gently and bluntly advance through the mesentery, manipulating the tissue without causing damage.
The surgeon grasps the tag on the left ureter, allowing it to pass smoothly through the mesenteric window created in the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon identifies the terminal ileum in the right abdomen and isolates a healthy segment of ileum approximately fifteen centimeters from the ileocecal junction.
The surgeon stretches the ileum from the retroperitoneum to the abdominal wall to estimate its length, ensuring it is adequate for the procedure.
The surgeon uses a sixty millimeter endovascular stapler to divide the ileal segment and its attached mesentery, visibly separating the tissue with precision.
The surgeon positions the endovascular stapler perpendicular to the anti-mesenteric border of the bowel, ensuring precise alignment for division.
The surgeon employs a technique that prevents devascularization of the ileal segment while ensuring adequate hemostasis of the mesentery during the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses scissors to open the anti-mesenteric ends of the ileal segment, ensuring the staple lines remain intact for further manipulation.
The surgeon aligns the anti-mesenteric portions of the bowel to create a bowel anastomosis, preparing to load the stapler for the procedure.
The surgeon positions the stapler into the bowel end and re-establishes bowel continuity in a side-to-side fashion along the anti-mesenteric border using the stapler.
The surgeon fires the stapler to complete the side-to-side bowel anastomosis, then assesses the integrity and length of the anastomosis before proceeding to close it with the bowel stapler.
The surgeon closes the bowel anastomosis using a transverse load from the stapler, ensuring the staple lines are intact and the continuity of the bowel is maintained.
The surgeon completes the bowel anastomosis by applying one or two additional 60mm staples to secure the anti-mesenteric portions of the bowel.
The surgeon reinforces the crotch of the bowel anastomosis with a 3-0 silk suture, then proceeds to close the mesenteric window.
The surgeon spatulates both ureters at their distal ends for approximately 2.5 centimeters, after tacking them to the pelvic side wall or having an assistant hold them under tension.
The surgeon uses a running four-row PDS suture to perform ureteral anastomosis employing the Wallace technique, ensuring precise alignment and secure connection of the ureters.
The surgeon secures the back wall of the ureters to the butt of the ileal conduit and places seven French urinary diversion stents into the ureters.
A 20 French council tip catheter is positioned in the pelvis, and stents are inserted over a 3.8 guide wire into the ureters.
The surgeon excises the distal staple line of the ileal conduit and passes the urinary diversion stents through the conduit.
The surgeon removes the unused portion of the distal ureter, which is visibly excised from the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the uretero-ileal anastomosis and proceeds to retroperitonealize the ileal conduit using interrupted II-O silk sutures, securing the conduit within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon anchors the urinary diversion stents to the ileal conduit using IV-O chromic sutures, securing the stents in place within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs dissection through the previously marked stomal site, carefully navigating through the fascia between the rectus muscle and into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a Babcock clamp to grasp the distal end of the ileal conduit along with the stents, pulling the conduit through the previously marked stoma site.
The surgical robot is undocked, and the stoma is created in a standard rosebud fashion, completing the external aspect of the ileal conduit.
The surgeon makes a small incision using a fan and steel technique to extract the specimen from the surgical site.
The surgeon removes the bladder and prostate specimen intact from the surgical field, completing the extraction process.
